title: O Mestre
blurb: Blood-red dyes on ivory silk. A sister’s shame. A genius stitching vengeance into every hem. Thirty days till the gala—and the one man who can cut Heitor Medeiros down stands stitching sequins in the dark.
comparable: For fans of *The Royal We* by Heather Cocks and Jessica Morgan, *The Devil Wears Prada* by Lauren Weisberger, and *The Lies of Locke Lamora* by Scott Lynch

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The fluorescent lights of Ateliê Encanto hummed like a swarm of bees trapped behind glass. Miguel Salgado kept his head down, fingers steady over the seam of a midnight-blue evening gown. The needle punctured fabric with the precision of a surgeon—clean, unhurried, invisible. Only the faintest tremor in his grip betrayed the anger beneath.

Across the room, Heitor Medeiros stood in a circle of admirers, his tailored suit catching the light like polished obsidian. He laughed at something the wardrobe assistant said, fingers tapping the gold cufflinks that marked his rise to Creative Director. The man hadn’t sewn a single stitch in years, but he wore the title like armor.

Miguel exhaled through his nose and pulled the thread taut. A secondary assistant hovered nearby, clipboard in hand, watching the clock tick toward quitting time.

“You missed a spot on the hem,” the assistant said, voice low.

Miguel didn’t look up. “The hem’s fine. The collar’s the issue.”

“Heitor just credited *Camila* for the *Amor Eterno* wardrobe. Called it her breakthrough.”

Miguel’s stitch paused. The needle hung a millimeter above the silk.

“Camila’s been here three months,” he said. “The sequins on the heroine’s ballgown? She glued them on crooked. I had to redo the whole thing.”

The assistant shifted. “Heitor said it was all her vision.”

Miguel’s jaw tightened. Across the room, Heitor adjusted the fit of his jacket, turning so the network logo on his lapel caught the light. The cameras in the ceiling whirred as they tracked his movement.

Miguel set the gown aside. “Where’s the original sketch for *Amor Eterno*?”

The assistant blinked. “In the archive. Why?”

“Because Heitor doesn’t know how to design a collar that doesn’t strangle the actor.”

A sharp laugh cut through the chatter. Heitor clapped a producer on the back, his voice carrying over the hum of the workshop. “You should’ve seen the look on her face when the dye ran! Classic.”

The room stilled for half a second.

Miguel’s hands stilled.

Heitor wasn’t talking about a wardrobe malfunction.

The assistant missed the shift in energy. “What dye?”

Miguel stood slowly, rolling his shoulders to ease the tension. “Nothing.”

He crossed the room without another word, weaving between racks of costumes, each one a silent witness to the games played in the name of art. The scent of aged fabric and mothballs filled his lungs. He paused at the back corner, behind a curtain of velvet drapes.

There, encased in glass, hung the bridal gown.

Valentina’s dress.

Miguel’s fingers hovered just shy of the case. The ivory silk shimmered under the LED lights, but something at the hem caught his eye—a glint, faint but deliberate. He leaned closer.

A stray thread.

Or not a thread.

It glowed.

A sliver of ultraviolet light leaked through the display, invisible to the naked eye but bright against the fibers. Miguel’s pulse steadied. He knew this effect. He’d designed it himself.

A signal.

Proof.

Miguel exhaled. The gala was in thirty days. The Master Designer title would be awarded based on live presentation, judged by a panel of network elites who cared more about spectacle than craft.

Heitor would never let him near the stage.

And yet.

Miguel’s fingers curled into a fist, then relaxed. He stepped back, but his gaze didn’t leave the gown.

“Miguel.”

The voice cut across the workshop.

Sofia Prado stood at the entrance, her dark curls catching the light, a leather satchel slung over one shoulder. A journalist, though not one who wrote about fashion.

She worked for *O Crime*, the investigative rag that exposed corruption in São Paulo’s elite circles.

Miguel nodded once. “Sofia.”

She tilted her head toward Heitor’s group. “Still letting him take the credit?”

Miguel picked up a dummy form, draped it with a half-finished costume. “It’s not about credit. It’s about control.”

Sofia stepped closer, lowering her voice. “Funny. I was going to say the same thing about my latest story.”

Miguel kept his hands busy. “You’re not here to talk about *Amor Eterno*.”

“No.” She pulled a folded sheet of paper from her bag. “Heitor’s not just stealing designs. He’s rewriting scripts. Altering contracts. He’s turning the network into a puppet theater.”

Miguel didn’t react. “That’s your lead.”

“Not anymore.” She slid the paper toward him, but he didn’t touch it.

“Why show me?”

“Because you’re the only one who can prove it.”

The fluorescent lights flickered.

Heitor’s laughter echoed through the room.

Miguel’s eyes flicked to the paper, then back to Sofia. “I don’t have anything to give you.”

She smiled, but it didn’t reach her eyes. “You always did.” Then she turned and walked out, the heels of her boots clicking against the cement floor.

Miguel didn’t watch her go.

He looked back at the gown.

The stray thread still glowed.

The fluorescent lights buzzed.

Chapter 2

The stage at Estúdio Aurora gleamed under spotlights so sharp they could cut glass. Gold scrollwork framed the set—a replica of a São Paulo mansion, complete with a sweeping staircase where the bride would make her grand entrance. The air smelled of hairspray, sweat, and the faint metallic tang of fresh paint.

Miguel adjusted the cuffs of his unremarkable blazer and kept his hands clasped behind his back. He wasn’t supposed to be here. Not officially. As head of costume design, his domain was Ateliê Encanto—rows of sewing machines, racks of fabric, the controlled chaos of creation. But tonight was *Amor Eterno*’s premiere, and premiere nights were political.

Heitor Medeiros stood center stage, microphone in hand, greeting the network’s elite like a royal puppet master. Producers, directors, actors in gowns that cost more than a year’s rent in Vila Madalena all clinked champagne flutes and laughed too loud.

Clara Vilela materialized at Miguel’s elbow, her smile tight. “You weren’t on the guest list.”

Miguel didn’t look at her. “No one checks the head of costume design.”

“They check tonight.” She adjusted her clipboard. “Heitor’s making an announcement after the first act. Something about ‘artistic vision.’”

Miguel’s fingers twitched. “He always does.”

Clara’s gaze flicked toward the wings. “He wants everyone to think he’s the genius behind *Amor Eterno*. All of it. The sets, the costumes, the damn lighting choices.”

A stagehand brushed past, nearly colliding with Miguel. The man carried a garment bag in one hand, his face flushed. Miguel caught the scent of cheap cologne and something sharper underneath—industrial dye.

“You,” Miguel said, stopping him. “That bag’s for Valentina Salgado?”

The man hesitated. “Yeah. Heitor said it had to be fresh. Right before she goes on.”

Miguel reached out, thumb and forefinger pinching the black nylon. “Open it.”

The man’s eyes darted toward the stage, where Heitor was now mid-laugh, his teeth too white in the harsh light. “I can’t. Heitor’s orders.”

Clara stepped between them. “Let him work, Miguel.”

Miguel didn’t let go. “Open. The. Bag.”

The stagehand swallowed and unzipped the bag. The scent hit Miguel first—sweet, chemical, wrong. Bright yellow tulle spilled out, the kind Valentina had chosen for her entrance dress. But the color was off. Too vibrant. Like neon under a blacklight.

Miguel’s stomach dropped.

Heitor’s voice rang out across the stage. “Ladies and gentlemen, a word before the show begins.”

Miguel dropped the bag and pushed past Clara. “Valentina,” he called to a passing actor. “Where’s Valentina?”

The actor blinked. “Getting into costume. Why?”

Miguel didn’t answer. He moved through the crowd, his path a straight line to the dressing rooms. The hallway smelled of hairspray and anxiety. A wardrobe assistant stood outside Valentina’s door, flipping through a checklist.

“Move,” Miguel said.

The assistant startled. “She’s not—”

“Move.”

He shoved past and threw open the door. The room was a riot of chiffon and lace, mirrors ringed with bulbs. Valentina stood at the center, her ivory dress already on a mannequin. She turned, her face pale beneath the makeup.

“Miguel? What are you doing here?”

He didn’t answer. He crossed the room in three strides and seized the gown. The fabric was warm, almost feverish to the touch. He held a sleeve up to the light.

Clara appeared in the doorway, breathless. “Miguel, for God’s sake, Heitor—”

“Heitor what?” Miguel’s voice was low, controlled. “Poisoned the dye again?”

Valentina’s fingers trembled. “What dye?”

Miguel turned the sleeve over. The yellow trim wasn’t yellow at all. Not naturally. It was a base layer of color—soft ivory, Valentina’s chosen shade—coated in something reactive. He rubbed his thumb over the hem. The dye transferred, sticking to his skin like tar.

“It’s UV-activated,” he said. “Heitor’s sabotaged you before the show even starts.”

Valentina’s breath hitched. “It can’t be fixed. Not in time.”

Miguel set the gown down. “It’s not about fixing it. It’s about proving it.”

Clara stepped forward, her voice sharp. “You can’t prove anything tonight. Heitor’s making an announcement. He’s saying *this* dress—the one that’s supposed to represent love and purity—was his vision. His design.”

Miguel didn’t look at her. He looked at Valentina. “You’re not wearing that.”

Valentina’s eyes welled. “I have to.”

“You don’t.” Miguel reached into his blazer and pulled out a folded length of ivory silk from his inner pocket. “I brought a spare. Plain. No dye. No tricks.”

Valentina stared at it. “Miguel, I can’t—”

“You can.” He pressed it into her hands. “Change.”

A stagehand rushed in, breathless. “Five minutes to curtain!”

Clara grabbed Miguel’s arm, her fingers digging in. “Heitor will lose his mind if she changes now. You know how he is.”

Miguel shook her off. “Heitor doesn’t get to decide what my sister wears.”

Clara’s jaw tightened. “This isn’t about the dress. It’s about control.”

Miguel met her gaze, unblinking. “Then let him choke on it.”

---

The auditorium fell silent as the curtain rose. Valentina stepped into the spotlight, her new gown catching the light like liquid ivory. The crowd murmured—not in horror, not yet, but in confusion. The gown wasn’t what they expected.

Heitor stood in the wings, his smile frozen.

Miguel remained in the shadows, arms crossed. Beside him, Sofia Prado stood with her arms crossed, a recorder in her hand. She didn’t look at him. Didn’t need to.

The scene played out as written. Valentina delivered her lines, her voice sure despite the tremor in her hands. The audience applauded politely.

Then the lights dimmed.

The romantic serenade began.

And Valentina’s dress changed.

It started at the hem—a faint shimmer, a shift from ivory to pale pink. Gasps rippled through the crowd. The color spread upward, staining the silk like blood in water. Within seconds, Valentina was bathed in a sickly crimson, her white dress now a grotesque parody of a bride in mourning.

The stage lights burned brighter.

The dye activated.

Heitor’s laughter cut through the stunned silence. “Ah, darling! How *passionate* of you!”

The crowd laughed nervously, unsure if this was part of the show.

Miguel’s hands curled into fists.

Valentina’s breath came fast. Her fingers clutched the folds of her dress, the poisonous dye seeping through the fabric, staining her skin. She swayed.

The audience gasped.

Miguel moved.

He stepped into the light, catching Valentina before her knees hit the stage. Her weight was slight, her body trembling. The crowd erupted in confusion and horror. Cameras flashed. Heitor’s face twisted—part delight, part panic.

Miguel didn’t look at him. He cradled Valentina against his chest, her head lolling against his shoulder. Her fingers twitched.

Then, the cold press of metal against his palm.

A USB drive.

Valentina’s lips moved against his ear—just once, barely a whisper.

“For you.”

Her eyes fluttered shut.

The stage went dark.

Chapter 3

The sewing lab smelled of aged oak and cotton dust. Miguel sat at his workbench under the single fluorescent bulb that would never flicker, never dim—never do anything but burn straight and true. Needle threaded, he began to stitch.

A single stitch.

Then another.

The rhythm steadied him. One. Two. Three. The thread pulled taut against the scrap of silk. The needle never wavered.

The door clicked.

He didn’t look up.

Clara Vilela stepped inside, her heels tapping on the concrete floor. She carried a leather portfolio, the kind used for script revisions. Her grip was too tight.

“You’re still here,” she said. “At nine o’clock on a Saturday.”

Miguel didn’t answer. He knotted the thread and set the scrap aside. Reached for another.

Clara hovered near the door, as if afraid to step farther. “The gala judges’ panel is locked. Heitor’s people made sure of it.”

Miguel pulled a spool of grey thread from the rack. Wound it onto the bobbin.

“Three months ago, the panel had open spots.” Clara’s voice was too bright. “Now? All six seats are filled with his allies. Or people he’s slept with. Or both.”

Miguel selected a bone-china thimble and slipped it onto his finger. The needle flashed as he drew it through the fabric.

“He’s making sure no one else gets a chance to speak,” Clara continued. “No dissent. No competition. Just his vision. Forever.”

The needle paused.

Clara exhaled through her nose. “You should be angry.”

Miguel picked up the fabric—a scrap of Valentina’s original dress, retrieved from the disposal bin after the premiere. The yellow fabric was stained now, faded where the UV dye had burned out. But the weave was still intact. The damage was only skin-deep.

“Heitor thinks you’re done,” Clara said. “After tonight—after you handed Valentina a new dress on a silver platter—he thinks you’ve accepted defeat.”

Miguel rethreaded the needle.

Clara stepped closer. “He’s celebrating tonight. At Solar Medeiros. His family’s mansion. He’s inviting half the network’s board. He wants them to see him as the future.”

The needle stabbed down. Pulled up.

Clara’s fingers tapped the portfolio. “I brought the final gala criteria. Seven pages of rules. No deviation allowed. Heitor wrote every one.”

Miguel set the needle down. The thread snapped at his touch.

Clara flinched.

Miguel stood. His chair rolled back without sound. He walked to the vault—a steel door inset into the wall behind the cutting table, its keypad glowing green. He pressed his palm against the scanner. The lock released with a quiet hiss.

Inside, on a padded shelf, lay the preserved wedding gown.

Clara hadn’t seen it yet.

She hadn’t been invited to see it.

Miguel lifted the gown from its glass case. The air moved with it—fabric that had never known a stage, a dress that had never been worn. The silk was untouched. The dye had been removed, molecule by molecule, fiber by fiber. Only the memory remained.

Clara’s breath caught. “Miguel…”

He turned to her. The gown draped over his arms, ivory and gold and delicate as a whisper.

Clara’s eyes flicked from the gown to his face. “You’re not mourning. You’re planning.”

Miguel said nothing.

Clara’s hands trembled. “Heitor thinks you’re broken. After tonight—after Valentina’s dress turned red on stage—he thinks you’re finished.”

Miguel folded the gown over his forearm. The silk rustled like a sigh.

Clara’s voice dropped. “He’s wrong, isn’t he?”

Miguel walked past her. The vault door locked behind him.

“Where are you going?” Clara called.

Miguel didn’t stop.

“Miguel!”

He pushed through the lab door into the dim hallway. The building was empty. The elevators were off. The air smelled of old coffee and ambition gone cold.

He took the stairs.

Clara followed, her portfolio clutched to her chest. “You can’t just—you can’t pretend this isn’t happening. Heitor’s not just going to hand you the title. He’s going to bury you.”

Miguel reached the ground floor. The exit door swung open to the São Paulo night—humid, alive, indifferent.

Clara grabbed his sleeve. “Talk to me.”

Miguel turned. The gown glowed faintly in the fluorescent light. “You had a chance,” he said. “You had a USB drive in your hand after the premiere. You could have given it to Sofia Prado right then. But you didn’t.”

Clara’s face paled.

“You still haven’t,” Miguel said. “Why?”

Clara’s fingers tightened on the portfolio. “You think I *wanted* to wait?”

“I think you wanted to see which way the wind blew.”

Clara’s jaw clenched. “Heitor promised me a production credit. My name on the credits. Something real.”

Miguel stepped closer. The silk brushed her arm. “And now?”

“And now I don’t know.” Clara’s voice cracked. “Heitor’s ruthless, but he’s not stupid. He knows what you’re capable of. That’s why he’s locking the panel. That’s why he smashed the mirror in his office last week.”

Miguel registered the detail without reaction. “You’ve been to Solar Medeiros.”

“I go where I’m invited.”

Miguel turned toward the street. A taxi idled at the curb, its engine purring.

Clara called after him, “Where are you taking that?”

Miguel opened the rear door. The gown’s train pooled on the seat like spilled moonlight.

Clara grabbed his wrist. “Heitor’s going to kill you.”

Miguel looked down at her hand. Then up at her face.

Clara let go.

Miguel got into the taxi. The door shut with a solid *thunk*.

Through the window, Clara’s face was a mask of regret and fear. The taxi pulled away.

The gown remained intact in the backseat.

No confession. No confrontation. No tears.

Just the quiet certainty of a man who had already decided what came next.

Chapter 4

Solar Medeiros loomed over the Jardins district like a mausoleum built for a king who refused to die. The mansion’s façade was all sharp angles and cold marble, its windows dark behind wrought-iron grates. Heitor Medeiros stood in the center of his office, surrounded by swatches of fabric pinned to a rotating stand. The colors shifted as the stand turned—emerald, scarlet, sapphire—each hue rich enough to taste.

His assistant, Lucas, hovered near the door, fingers twitching like he was counting invisible stitches.

“These are the tones for the new season,” Heitor said, not looking up. “Rich. Luxurious. Unmistakable.”

One of the junior designers, a woman in a sharp blazer, nodded too quickly. “The press will call it your signature.”

Heitor smiled. It didn’t reach his eyes. “They’ll say it’s revolutionary.”

Lucas cleared his throat. “The invitations went out this morning. Half the board confirmed. The others? They’ll come.”

Heitor plucked a swatch from the stand—the scarlet—and let it drift between his fingers. “Good. Let them see what happens when a Medeiros sets the tone.”

The junior designer’s smile faltered. “About the writers who quit—”

Heitor’s hand stilled. “They lacked vision.”

“But the scripts for *Amor de Ouro*—”

“Are now *my* problem.” Heitor dropped the swatch onto a marble side table. It landed with a soft *thud*. “Get out.”

The junior designer didn’t hesitate. She left, closing the door so quietly it might not have happened.

Lucas exhaled. “The color lab delivered the first batch of prototypes. They’re…”

Heitor turned. The light caught the gold ring on his right hand—the one with the Medeiros crest. “They’re what?”

“Flawed.” Lucas swallowed. “The dye didn’t take evenly. Some threads absorbed too much. Others not enough.”

Heitor walked to the desk. A framed photo sat there, glass slightly fogged with age. It showed him and Miguel at a fundraiser two years ago, smiling like allies. Like friends.

He picked it up.

Lucas’s voice wavered. “Should I call the lab?”

Heitor’s thumb pressed against the glass. The frame cracked.

Lucas flinched.

Heitor didn’t. He turned the frame over and tapped the back until the glass shattered. Shards rained onto the desk, glinting like broken promises.

Lucas stared at the mess. “Heitor, please—”

“Leave.” Heitor’s voice was calm. A hurricane is calm when it’s still a thousand miles away.

Lucas left.

The door clicked shut.

Heitor picked up a shard of glass. The reflection in it was distorted—his face stretched and warped. He set the shard down carefully, as if it were made of something precious. Then he reached into his pocket and pulled out a small vial of clear liquid.

He uncapped it.

Raised it toward the light.

A single drop fell onto the desk. It landed near the shattered photo. The glass shard sat in it, half-submerged.

The drop spread.

It seeped into the grain of the wood, darkening it slightly, like a bruise blooming under skin.

Heitor watched.

No sound. No movement.

Just the slow creep of the liquid.

Then—

A sharp knock at the door.

Heitor didn’t react. The knock came again, insistent.

“Come in,” he said.

The door opened. A maid stood there, her uniform crisp, her hands clasped. “Senhor Medeiros. Your guests are arriving. The board members. The directors. They’re in the grand hall.”

Heitor didn’t look at her. “Tell them I’ll be down shortly.”

The maid hesitated. “Shall I have the champagne served?”

Heitor finally looked up. His eyes were cold. “Only the best for my guests.”

The maid left.

Heitor picked up another shard of glass. Examined it. Then he slid it into his pocket, pressing it against the vial in his other hand.

He stood. Adjusted his cuffs.

Walked to the mirror.

His reflection stared back—hands steady, jaw set, eyes sharp.

He touched the ring on his finger.

Turned away just as the door opened again.

This time, it was his sister, Isabel.

“Heitor,” she said, her voice cool. “The party’s starting. You’re not hiding in here all night.”

Heitor smoothed his jacket. “I was reviewing the evening’s agenda.”

Isabel stepped inside, her heels clicking on the marble. “You’ve been reviewing it for an hour. The board is restless. They want to see you.”

Heitor walked past her. “Then let them see.”

Isabel followed. “You’re in a mood.”

Heitor didn’t answer.

Isabel’s gaze flicked to the desk—the shattered photo, the dark stain spreading like ink. She didn’t comment.

They walked down the hall, past portraits of Medeiros ancestors, past suits of armor standing sentinel. The grand hall was already filling—network executives in tailored suits, directors with sharp smiles, actors laughing too loud.

Heitor took a flute of champagne from a passing tray.

Raised it.

The room stilled.

“To the future,” he said.

The guests repeated it. Their glasses clinked.

Heitor drank.

The liquid was cold. Perfect.

Then he felt it.

A whisper.

A shift in the air.

His fingers tightened around the glass.

Something was wrong.

He looked down.

His cuff was damp.

Not with champagne.

With something darker.

And it wasn’t spreading evenly.

It was bleeding.

Unevenly.

Like the dye.

Chapter 5

Estúdio Aurora smelled like popcorn and ambition. The air conditioning hummed, fighting against the heat of a hundred bodies crammed into the preview theater. Network executives perched in the first three rows, their jewelry glinting under the harsh overhead lights. Behind them, the crew stood in loose clusters, whispering about the new season’s palette. The color samples were already laid out on a long table at the front, draped in silks so deep they looked edible—sangria red, midnight blue, burnt gold.

Heitor Medeiros stood at the podium, his back to the screen, his posture relaxed but precise. His suit was charcoal today, the lapels sharp enough to cut glass. In his hand, he held a single swatch of fabric the size of a playing card. He raised it like a toast.

“This,” he said, “is tomorrow.”

A ripple of applause. Not thunderous, but attentive.

Heitor unfurled the swatch. The fabric cascaded over his fingers, shimmering. “A single hue that shifts under stage lights. Rich when the camera’s warm. Cool when it’s cold. Alive.”

One of the junior producers leaned toward a colleague. “Is this real? Or is it VFX?”

Heitor heard. He turned the swatch toward the light. “Real. Every thread dyed to our exacting standards.”

The camera operator in the back zoomed in. The fabric’s weave caught the lens—tiny, perfect knots, nothing out of place.

Heitor let the swatch drop. It landed on the table with a whisper. “This is the future of Rede Estrela Dourada.”

A beat. Then a polite round of clapping.

Heitor smiled. “And now, the moment you’ve been waiting for.”

He raised a hand. The lights dimmed.

A hush fell.

The first sample rolled onto the stage on a motorized stand. A full-length bolt of fabric descended from the rafters, unfurling like a flag. The color was a deep, velvety plum—not just a color, but a mood. Sultry. Dangerous. Elegant.

Heitor stepped forward. “This is *Noite sem Fim*—‘Night Without End.’ The lead telenovela. The lead actress wears this in every scene.”

The room leaned in.

Heitor gestured to the lab tech waiting by the side curtain. “Bring out the prototype.”

The tech wheeled a mannequin forward. A dress of the same plum fabric draped its form, fitted to the waist, flared at the hips—a gown designed for drama, for close-ups, for tears.

The mannequin stopped in the center of the stage.

Heitor spread his hands. “Behold.”

The dress shimmered under the lights.

Then the first streak appeared.

A thin line, vertical, near the hem. Like a vein of darker dye.

Silence.

The line widened. Spread. The plum fabric bloomed with irregular splotches—some lighter, some streaked like rust.

Heitor didn’t move. His face stayed calm.

The room didn’t breathe.

The streaks deepened. The fabric began to shimmer unevenly, the threads catching the light in jagged waves. The effect was no longer luxurious. It was broken.

Someone in the front row gasped.

Heitor’s fingers twitched at his sides. Just once.

Then the lab tech, sweating now, reached out. Touched the fabric.

His fingers came away stained purple-black.

“Oh God,” he whispered.

Heitor’s voice cut through the air like a scalpel. “Take it away.”

The tech recoiled. The mannequin rolled offstage. The fabric sample was stripped from the stand and carried out in a wad, dripping.

Heitor didn’t follow it with his eyes.

He stared straight ahead.

The lights flickered back to full.

The executives were on their phones, murmuring. The director of *Noite sem Fim* stood up, face flushed. “This is a disaster. We can’t shoot in this.”

Heitor raised a hand. “Wait.”

He turned. Walked to the podium. Picked up the fallen swatch from the table. Examined it.

His jaw tightened.

Lucas appeared beside him, pale. “Senhor Medeiros, I—I can call the lab. Have them reprocess—”

Heitor crushed the swatch in his fist. Dye powder sifted through his fingers. “No.”

“But the gala—we need the showcase in three weeks—”

Heitor’s gaze locked onto the empty stage, the dark stains still visible on the floorboards where the dress had dripped.

Then he looked at his hand. At the dye clinging to his skin. At the way it darkened unevenly, like ink bleeding through paper.

A slow, cold realization settled in his chest.

He wasn’t angry.

He was afraid.

Heitor dropped the swatch. Turned to the room.

“Cancel the presentation,” he said. “We’ll review internally.”

The director spluttered. “Cancel? But Heitor, the board expects—”

“The board will understand.” His voice was calm. Dead calm. “This is not a public issue. Not yet.”

They heard the emphasis. Not yet.

The room thinned out quickly. The executives filed out, shoulders tense, phones pressed to ears. The crew lingered, stealing glances at the dark stains on the stage.

Heitor stood alone, hands clenched, staring at nothing.

Lucas hovered. “Should I have security clean the stage?”

Heitor didn’t answer.

His hands were trembling.

Just slightly.

Just enough.

Chapter 6

Sofia Prado’s inbox pinged twice in fifteen seconds. She minimized her investigative notes on a telenovela bribery ring and clicked over.

The attachment loaded slow. Corporate memos from Clara Vilela: color palettes, fabric swatches, delivery delays at Ateliê Encanto. Sofia scrolled. Something about a dye lot.

Then a second file. Unencrypted. Raw.

A script.

Title: *Noite sem Fim*—“Night Without End.”
Scene 47. A conversation between the lead actress and her lover. Lines identical to a short film from five years ago, credited to an indie director in Rio.

Sofia opened her burner phone. Created a new email. Typed:

From: anonyme@anonime.net
To: curiosity@sofiaprado.com

Subject: Telenovela’s Dark Secret—Bridal Dress Bombshell Inside.

She attached the script. Typed two more lines:

Heitor Medeiros copied entire scenes from a banned film. The dye failure at the gala wasn’t sabotage—it was plagiarism exposure waiting to happen.

Hit send.

A tap on her shoulder. The barista at Mirante do Café held a cortado. “Your usual, Sofia.”

“Thanks, Leo.”

She turned back to her screen. The email sat in Outbox. Unread.

Sofia leaned back. Stared at the screen. The attachment icon pulsed once, twice. Then gone.

Her phone buzzed.

Unknown number.

Message: You’ll want to see this.

She clicked. A photo. A manila folder labeled “V. Salgado Wedding Exhibit.” Inside: a red gown behind glass. Faint yellow threads woven into the hem.

Sofia’s thumb hovered. She zoomed. Wrote back:

Who sent this?

The reply came instantly: Someone who remembers the truth.

She exhaled. Took a sip of coffee. The cortado was cold.

---

Clara Vilela’s desk at Ateliê Encanto faced a wall of color cards. She swiveled in her chair, phone pressed to ear.

“It wasn’t me,” she said. “I didn’t send anything.”

Her assistant held up a printout. “Incoming firewall alert. Unauthorized file transfer from your terminal at 2:17 p.m.”

Clara’s fingers tightened on the phone. “I was in the dye lab at 2:17.”

“Then someone used your login.”

“Change the password. Now.”

She hung up. Stared at the alert. Red push notification: “External breach detected.”

Clara minimized the window. Opened a private folder. Scripts. Old ones. Leaks. Evidence.

Nothing about *Noite sem Fim*. Nothing about the wedding.

She rolled her shoulders. Grabbed a highlighter. Scanned the latest vendor invoice for lace trim.

The phone buzzed again. Unknown number.

She hesitated. Opened the message.

A photo: her login screen, timestamp 2:17 p.m. Her face reflected in the monitor.

Clara’s pulse jumped. She closed the message. Deleted it.

Then she opened her browser. Typed an address. Waited for the page to load.

A dark site. Password protected. Prompt: “Access code?”

She typed quickly: *aurora2024*

The vault opened.

Inside: a single video file. Wedding reception. Valentina Salgado in her gown. Mid-dance. A red stain blossoming on the skirt.

Clara clicked. Watched the hem flare scarlet.

She closed the file. Swallowed.

Then she minimized everything. Cleared cache. Emptied recycle bin.

A shadow fell across her desk.

Lucas Oliveira stood there, arms crossed. His tie loose, collar unbuttoned.

“Heitor wants the dye analysis by five,” he said. “The lab’s still cleaning the stage.”

Clara didn’t look up. “I’ll send it.”

“You okay?”

“Fine.”

Lucas didn’t move. “Funny. You look like you’ve seen a ghost.”

Clara finally met his eyes. Smiled. “Just tired.”

He nodded once. Walked away.

She waited five seconds. Then tapped a new email.

To: anonyme@anonime.net
Subject: Re: Telenovela’s Dark Secret—Bridal Dress Bombshell Inside.

I don’t know what you’re playing at. Stop.

She hit send. Watched it vanish into the network.

Five minutes later, her screen flickered. A new message.

Subject: Re: Re: Telenovela’s Dark Secret—Bridal Dress Bombshell Inside.

You’ll change your mind.

Clara closed the laptop. Locked it in her drawer.

Then she stood. Walked to the staff bathroom. Locked the door.

And for the first time in years, she let herself cry.

Chapter 7

Mirante do Café smelled of sugar and gossip. Espresso machines hissed. Waiters carried trays of pastel de nata between tables where writers, directors, and costume designers hunched over laptops and cellphones. The air hummed with the low murmur of deals and betrayals, the currency of Rede Estrela Dourada.

Heitor Medeiros sat at the corner booth, back to the wall, a cortado untouched in front of him. His suit was charcoal, his cufflinks silver. He sipped water, swirled it slowly, watching the ice melt without touching the cup.

Across from him, Dona Lúcia Oliveira adjusted her hat, a wide-brimmed thing in emerald green that matched the color card pinned to her chest. She tapped her pen against the gossip column’s notepad.

“Heitor,” she said, “I’ve been trying to get you alone for weeks.”

He didn’t look up. “You have me now.”

“Why hasn’t Miguel Salgado been seen at the ateliers lately? Color me curious.”

The table went quiet. Not the polite kind—the kind where breath hitches and forks pause mid-bite. The café didn’t get louder. It didn’t need to. The silence itself was the answer.

Heitor set the water glass down. The ice clinked. “Miguel’s been focusing on private projects. He’s always had a habit of disappearing when deadlines loom.”

Dona Lúcia scribbled. “Funny. I heard he’s been seen at Solar Medeiros. At odd hours.”

Heitor’s smile didn’t reach his eyes. “Rumors travel faster than fabric swatches.”

She leaned in. “And the gown? The one that turned red in front of half of São Paulo? Any updates on the investigation?”

A waiter approached. Heitor waved him off with a flick of his fingers. The waiter retreated like a shadow.

“Clara’s handling it,” Heitor said. “She’s thorough.”

Dona Lúcia’s pen hovered. “Clara’s been hacked, Heitor. Again. Someone’s been in her terminal. And not just files—scripts. Footage.”

Heitor’s jaw tightened. Just for a second. Then his face smoothed. “Clara’s login is public knowledge. She should know better than to leave it unsecured.”

“She logged in at 2:17 p.m. the day the footage was sent to a journalist.”

Heitor’s fingers tapped the table once. A rhythm. A signal.

Dona Lúcia flipped her notebook shut. “You’re not worried?”

“Should I be?”

She stood. The emerald hat tilted as she looked down at him. “Heitor, dear, when the bride’s dress turns red on live television, when a journalist starts digging into plagiarized scripts, and when your best friend’s login gets hijacked—maybe it’s time to worry.”

Heitor didn’t answer. He picked up his cortado. Took a sip. The foam stuck to his lip. He wiped it away with a napkin, slow and deliberate.

“You have a good day, Dona Lúcia.”

She adjusted her hat. “I’ll be back.”

The cafe’s murmur swallowed her as she walked out. Heitor stayed motionless, staring at the foam ring on the tablecloth. The ice had melted completely.

His phone buzzed once on the table. A text lit up the screen.

*Someone’s asking about the bride’s dress. UV evidence confirmed.*

Heitor exhaled through his nose. One long breath. Then he touched the screen. The message vanished.

He put the phone in his pocket. Stood. Left the cortado half-finished.

Outside, the São Paulo afternoon was a blur of honking cars and shifting light. Heitor walked toward his car, his driver already opening the door. He slid in without a word.

The car pulled away. Heitor stared out the window, watching the city go by. The skyline. The billboards. The faces in the crowd, all of them actors in someone else’s story.

His phone buzzed again in his pocket. A second text. A photo this time.

A close-up of fabric. Yellow threads woven into red. Faint UV glow in the dark.

His jaw clenched. The photo disappeared before he could save it.

Heitor leaned back. Closed his eyes.

The car turned onto Rua Augusta. The GPS announced their destination: Estúdio Aurora.

Heitor opened his eyes. The photo was still in his mind.

He reached into his pocket. Pulled out a small device—an UV flashlight. Thumbed the switch.

The light flickered. Then steadied.

He clicked it off. Put it away.

The car stopped at a red light. Heitor watched the pedestrians cross the street. A woman in a yellow dress. A man in a suit. A child holding a balloon.

None of them cared.

None of them knew.

The light turned green.

The car moved forward.

Heitor’s phone buzzed a third time.

He didn’t look. Didn’t answer.

The car accelerated.

And the light in the rearview mirror—flickered once—like a dying heartbeat.

The backdoor of Solar Medeiros smelled of lemon polish and bad decisions. Heitor stood in the dim hallway, his shoes silent on the marble, his phone pressed to his ear.

“Get me figures,” he said. “All of them. Every account that’s been touched.”

Heitor didn’t lower the phone. “I didn’t. But since you’re here, tell me why the costume workshop shows three unapproved fabric purchases under my name.”

Her fingers tightened around the bannister. “I didn’t authorize them.”

“Then who did?”

“I don’t know.”

“That’s not a useful answer.”

She swallowed. “Heitor, I—I just found out the payments were rerouted to a shell account in Paraguay.”

Heitor smiled. Just the edges of his mouth. “You’ve been with me six months. Paraguay shouldn’t be news.”

“But you’re quitting.”

Her breath hitched. “Yes.”

“Today.”

“By noon.”

Heitor nodded, as if she’d just confirmed a minor scheduling conflict. “Thank you for the honesty.”

She didn’t move. “You’re not going to ask why?”

“You’re leaving because you’re afraid. Fear makes people careless, and carelessness is expensive.” He tapped the phone against his palm. “Go. Before you make it worse.”

She took a step back. Then another. Then she was gone, her heels clicking down the hall like a countdown.

Heitor exhaled. Breathed in. The scent of lemon faded, replaced by something older—dust in the air ducts, the ghost of last night’s cigar smoke.

His phone buzzed. A notification from the studio’s PR feed:

*Breaking: Exclusive guest list for Rede Estrela Dourada’s “Master Designer” gala leaked to Folha da Tarde. Who’s on it? Full breakdown inside.*

Heitor clicked the link. The page loaded. Names scrolled past. Producers, directors, costume legends. And then—

*Valentina Salgado.*

His jaw locked. The screen glowed pale blue in the dim hallway.

A second notification popped up:

*Sofia Prado, investigative journalist, accuses Creative Director Heitor Medeiros of sabotaging Valentina Salgado’s wedding to protect “artistic purity.”*

The headline hung in the air, weightless and venomous.

Heitor’s thumb hovered over the screen. He could delete it. Could call IT. Could pretend he hadn’t seen.

Instead, he forwarded the link to his private number. Typed a reply to the sender:

*Get it taken down.*

Sent.

Then he pocketed the phone. Turned toward his office.

The lights flickered—once, twice—like a warning before the storm.

Heitor didn’t pause. He reached the door, swiped his card, and stepped inside.

The office was dark except for the glow of three monitors. His PR team was already there, huddled around a laptop, their faces lit by the screen’s harsh light.

“Heitor,” said the lead PR woman, Lídia, without turning. “We need to talk.”

He didn’t answer. He crossed to the window. São Paulo sprawled below, a grid of ambition and neon, indifferent to scandals and sabotage.

Lídia cleared her throat. “Folha’s running with Sofia Prado’s piece. It’s going viral. The hashtag #RedBride is trending.”

“So?”

“Guilt is a state of mind, Lídia. Evidence is something else.” He turned. The monitors cast shadows across his face. “Where’s the rebuttal?”

“We’re drafting one now. But the photos—”

“What photos?”

She hesitated. Then she tapped a key. The screen shifted. A collage appeared: Valentina in her ruined gown, the red dye blooming like a wound; Heitor at his desk the day before the wedding, signing off on fabric tests; a close-up of the gown’s seam, loose threads and uneven stitching.

Heitor’s fingers twitched.

“Those are fabricated,” he said.

“They’re in the cloud. And they’re tagged.”

“By who?”

“Anonymous.”

Heitor walked to the desk. Looked down at the keyboard. The keys were warm.

“Someone’s feeding them,” he said. “Someone inside.”

Lídia didn’t argue. She rarely did.

Heitor picked up a pen. Rolled it between his fingers. The metal was cold.

“Shut down the cloud copy. Burn the drafts. Everything.” He looked at his team. “I want every device sanitized within the hour. No backups. No traces.”

The team moved. Keyboards clattered. Phones buzzed with frantic texts.

Heitor turned back to the window. The skyline pulsed with billboards—bright, meaningless faces selling soap and dreams.

His phone buzzed again. A text from an unknown number:

*You should’ve burned the dress.*

Heitor’s pulse stuttered. He didn’t open the message. Didn’t acknowledge it.

Instead, he reached into his pocket. Pulled out the UV flashlight. Thumbed the switch.

The beam cut through the dark like a scalpel.

He swept it across the floor, the desk, the monitors. No glow. No threads. No proof.

He clicked it off. Put it away.

The office lights flickered again—longer this time. A deep, shuddering dim.

Lídia looked up. “Heitor?”

The power died.

The screens went black. The air conditioner groaned. Then silence.

Darkness swallowed the room.

Heitor didn’t move. Didn’t speak. The dark was a living thing, pressing against his skin.

Then—a sound. A knock. Three quick raps on the door.

Lídia’s breath hitched. “Heitor?”

He didn’t answer. He stepped forward, hands outstretched, until his fingers met the doorframe.

The knock came again. From inside the office.

Heitor froze.

The knock repeated. Insistent. Familiar.

He reached for the door handle. Turned it.

The door swung open.

Light spilled in from the hall. Lídia’s face was pale, her eyes wide.

“Heitor,” she whispered. “The door was locked from the inside.”

Chapter 9

She didn’t knock.

“You shouldn’t be here,” he said.

Clara shut the door behind her. The lock clicked into place with finality. “We both know that’s not true.”

“Yes.”

“Not all of them.” Clara’s eyes flicked to the corner of the lab. A single white cable snaked from the outlet to a small black device no bigger than a pack of cards. “That’s a live feed. Sent to Sofia’s burner phone. She’s been recording every word in this room since I walked in.”

Miguel didn’t look. “You’re lying.”

*Miguel Salgado acknowledges Heitor Medeiros’ decisive role in uncovering the threat to Rede Estrela Dourada’s premiere.*

No signature line. No date. Just the words, the threat, the trap.

Miguel dropped the envelope onto the workbench. It landed with a soft *thud*.

“I won’t.”

Clara’s mouth thinned. “You don’t have a choice.”

“I always have a choice.”

She stepped closer. Close enough that he smelled her perfume—bergamot and something clinical, like hospital soap. “You’re going to lose the gown. You’re going to lose the gala. You’re going to lose everything. And Heitor? He’ll frame you. He’ll make it look like you were the saboteur all along. He’s already planting evidence.”

Miguel reached for his scissors. Not the surgical ones—the heavy tailor’s shears with the black handles. He opened them. The blades gleamed under the lab’s fluorescent lights.

Clara’s eyes flicked to the scissors. “You wouldn’t.”

“I already did.” He closed them with a sharp *snap*. “Years ago. Before any of this. Before you. Before Heitor. Before the gown.”

Her breath hitched. For the first time, uncertainty flickered across her face. “What are you talking about?”

Clara’s voice sharpened. “Miguel. Stop.”

He flipped the switch.

The lab went dark. The emergency lights kicked in, casting everything in a sickly red glow. The scissors in his hand gleamed like a blade in the gloom.

Clara’s heels clattered against the concrete as she took a step back. “What are you *doing*?”

Miguel didn’t answer. He reached under the workbench. Pressed his palm against a small red button flush with the metal.

A deep *hum* filled the air. Not the lab’s generator—the sound of something else. Something hidden.

Clara’s eyes darted to the ceiling. To the walls. To the door. “What button did you just press?”

Miguel didn’t look at her. “The one that calls the fire department.”

“That’s a fire alarm.”

“No.” He pressed it again. The hum grew louder. “That’s the silent alarm. The one that triggers the security cameras. The police trace. The network lock-down.”

Clara’s face paled. “You wouldn’t.”

“I would.” He turned to face her. The red light painted half his face in shadow. “You want to know why Sofia’s recording is useless? Because the second that alarm triggers, her burner phone bricks. No signal. No recording. Just a brick in her pocket.”

Clara’s hands clenched. “You’re bluffing.”

Miguel pressed the button a third time. The hum vibrated through the floor.

Clara’s phone buzzed in her pocket. She flinched. Pulled it out. Screen cracked. Black. No signal.

Her breath came fast. “You bastard.”

Miguel dropped the scissors onto the workbench. They landed with a metallic clang. “No. That’s Heitor.”

Clara stuffed the dead phone back into her pocket. Her blazer was unbuttoned. Her blouse was untucked. She looked like a woman who had just run out of options.

“What now?” she asked.

Miguel walked to the door. Pressed his palm against the scanner. The green light blinked. The lock disengaged.

Clara didn’t move. “You’re letting me walk out?”

He didn’t look back. “I don’t need to trap you. You’re already trapped.”

The door hissed open. The hallway beyond was empty. Silent. The red glow from the exit sign bled into the dark.

Clara hesitated. Then she stepped forward. Her shoulder brushed Miguel’s as she passed. The contact lasted a second. A breath.

Miguel didn’t flinch.

She paused at the door. “They’re not going to stop coming for you.”

“I know.”

“Heitor’s already sending the cleanup crew.”

“I know.”

Clara turned. Her eyes met his in the dim light. “You can still stop this. Before it’s too late.”

Miguel didn’t answer.

She walked out. The door swung shut behind her. The lock clicked into place.

Miguel waited five full seconds. Then he pressed the red button again.

The hum died. The emergency lights flickered. Then went out.

Darkness swallowed the lab.

Miguel exhaled. Crossed to the workbench. Picked up the envelope Clara had left. Didn’t open it. Just held it in his palm, feeling the weight of the lies inside.

From the far corner of the lab, a single red diode blinked. Silent. Watching.

Chapter 10

Mirante do Café sat on Rua Augusta like a jewel in a velvet box—mirrors on every wall, chrome everywhere, the kind of place where people came to see and be seen. Sofia Prado chose a corner booth where the light hit just right, so her face stayed half in shadow. She’d already ordered two espressos. The second cup had gone cold.

Miguel walked in at seven-fifteen, coat damp from the sudden evening shower, a navy umbrella tucked under his arm. The hostess barely glanced at him. He didn’t need an escort. Everyone in the building knew his face, even if they didn’t recognize the hands that shaped their favorite costumes.

He spotted Sofia immediately. She didn’t wave. Just tilted her chin toward the seat across from her.

Miguel sat. The vinyl squeaked.

“You’re late,” Sofia said.

“You’re early.”

She slid a manila folder across the table. Inside, black-and-white prints of the gala gown—every seam, every stitch, every microscopic imperfection. “That’s not the dress from the wedding.”

Miguel didn’t touch it. “It’s my work.”

“And Heitor’s sabotage didn’t just ruin a wedding. It exposed a network. A system. A *business*.”

The café’s chatter wrapped around them like smoke. A waiter passed with a tray of pastéis, the scent of fried dough and cinnamon cutting through the bitterness. Miguel’s stomach growled. He ignored it.

“You think I planned the sabotage?” Miguel asked.

“Didn’t you?”

He set the cup down. “What do *you* think?”

Sofia leaned forward. Her fingers hovered over the photos. “I think you’re smarter than Heitor. More obsessive. More *perfect*. He builds empires on stolen ideas. You build armor. You don’t just sew—you *engineer*.”

Miguel watched a couple at the next table argue over a shared dessert. The man cut the slice unevenly. The woman’s fork scraped her plate in protest.

“Heitor poisoned my sister’s gown,” Miguel said. “He turned her wedding into a spectacle. He made sure the whole city laughed while she cried.”

Sofia didn’t flinch. “And you let it happen?”

Sofia’s eyes flicked to the camera. “You were there?”

“Indirectly.” Miguel tapped the camera twice. The tiny green light blinked. “You’ve seen the UV footage. The dress turned red on live TV. But what you *haven’t* seen is the ultraviolet thread woven into the hem. It wasn’t just a signal. It was a trap.”

Sofia exhaled through her nose. “You lured him into exposing himself.”

“Yes.”

She picked up the camera. Turned it over in her hands. “And when did you decide to tell me?”

“Now.”

She laughed—a sharp, humorless sound. “You don’t trust me. Not after Clara.”

Sofia’s pen clicked. She pulled out a tiny digital recorder, pressed record. “And what about you?”

Miguel looked at the camera. At the cold light in Sofia’s eyes. “The truth is a gown that changes color under UV light. A dye formula only I can replicate. A chain of evidence that leads straight to Heitor’s door.”

Sofia stopped the recorder. “You need me to publish this.”

She went still. “What does that mean?”

Miguel leaned in. His voice dropped to a murmur. “You write the story. You expose Heitor’s crimes. And you make sure the public knows *why* it happened. Not just the corruption. The *artistry*. The sabotage. The fact that a designer—one they’ve ignored for years—outsmarted the system.”

Sofia’s pen hovered over her notebook. “You want credit.”

Sofia picked it up. Weighed it in her palm. “Heitor will know it came from you.”

“He already knows.”

She looked at him. Really looked. Not as a journalist. Not as a rival. As a woman who’d studied a man’s hands for two hours and still couldn’t read him.

“You’re not afraid,” she said.

“Of what?”

Miguel picked up the cold espresso. Took a sip. Swallowed. “I’m not afraid of Heitor.”

“Then what are you afraid of?”

The café’s lights flickered. A bulb in the chandelier died with a pop. The world outside the windows blurred—rain streaking the glass like tears.

Miguel set the cup down. “I’m afraid of being too late.”

Sofia didn’t react. Just pocketed the USB drive. The recorder clicked off.

Then she reached across the table. Not for his hand. For the camera.

Miguel let her take it.

She held it up. Examined it. “Heitor’s already raided Clara’s office. Swept her apartment. Burned her devices. If this footage is as good as you say—”

Sofia didn’t get up. “Where are you going?”

“Not a trap.” Miguel buttoned his coat. “A scope.”

He left without looking back. The café’s door chimed as it swung shut behind him. The rain had stopped. The sidewalk glistened under the neon signs of Rua Augusta.

Sofia sat alone in the booth. The recorder’s red light blinked. The USB drive in her pocket felt like a grenade with the pin pulled.

She pressed record again.

Chapter 11
