She moved to the worktable, set her coat down. Her hands were steady. “Heitor already knows about the gown. About the dye. About the UV thread. He’s had people crawling over my apartment for three days.”

Miguel finally turned in his chair. “And you let him?”

Clara’s lips thinned. “I didn’t give him anything. But I didn’t stop him, either.”

“That’s not protection.”

“It’s survival.” She picked up a spool of silver thread, rolled it between her fingers. “You think I wanted to withhold that script? You think I didn’t want to burn Heitor’s empire to the ground the second I saw what was in it?” She dropped the thread. It unraveled across the table. “But when I went to Sofia, when I told her what I knew—Heitor’s people were already there. They intercepted the message before it even left the server.”

Miguel watched her. She wasn’t lying. But she wasn’t telling the whole truth, either.

Clara exhaled through her nose. “They fired me this morning. Formal complaint. Breach of confidentiality. Unprofessional conduct.” She laughed, a short, brittle sound. “Funny how ‘unprofessional’ only applies when you cross Heitor.”

Miguel swiveled his chair to face her. “How much did they take?”

“Everything. My laptop. My backups. My research on the new season’s wardrobe.” Her eyes flicked to the corner of the lab where Miguel’s own workstation sat, untouched. “But they didn’t touch yours.”

Miguel didn’t react. “Heitor’s getting desperate.”

“He’s not desperate. He’s *winning*.” Clara’s voice cracked. Just once. “He’s dismantling the network piece by piece. First the gown, then the premiere, then the audit on his family’s accounts. And now me. Next, it’s you. And after that?” She gestured vaguely toward the door. “After that, it’s anyone who stands in his way.”

Miguel stood. Walked to the far side of the lab, where the ultraviolet lamp hung above a workbench. He flipped the switch. The lamp buzzed to life, casting a faint violet glow over the table.

Clara followed him. “What are you doing?”

Miguel picked up a scrap of fabric from the bench—one of Heitor’s failed samples from the canceled premiere. He held it under the light.

Nothing.

No glow. No change. Just dull, lifeless beige.

He dropped it back onto the table. Turned off the lamp.

Clara crossed her arms. “You’re still planning something.”

Miguel didn’t answer.

She stepped closer. “Heitor’s going to find out. He’s already got half the network looking for you. And Sofia? She’s publishing her article tomorrow. Once it goes live, Heitor won’t just be angry. He’ll be *dangerous*.”

Miguel turned back to his monitors. The news feed had cut to an anchor discussing Valentina’s interview. The screen flickered. The broadcast glitched.

Clara’s phone buzzed. She pulled it out, glanced at the screen, and paled.

Miguel didn’t ask what it said.

Clara slipped the phone back into her pocket. Her hands were shaking now. “Heitor knows about Valentina’s footage. The uncut one. The one that shows *exactly* what happened to her dress.”

Miguel kept his voice even. “Heitor already knows what happened.”

“Not like this.” Clara’s breath hitched. “He’s got a team at her safe house. They’re going to take it. Burn it. Make sure nothing survives.”

Miguel’s fingers curled into fists. For the first time in years, he felt the old rage coil in his chest—not hot, not explosive, but cold, precise, *calculating*.

Clara continued, voice rising now. “Heitor’s not just covering his tracks. He’s erasing the past. If he gets that footage, he can rewrite history. The dress turns red? It was an accident. The dye was faulty. The sabotage? Never happened.”

Miguel turned off the monitor. The lab fell into silence.

Clara took a step back. “What are you going to do?”

Miguel walked to the back of the lab, where the safe was embedded into the wall. He spun the dial, the numbers clicking into place. The door swung open. Inside, under layers of protective fabric, lay the original bridal gown—the one he had sewn in secret, the one Heitor had tried to destroy.

It was still white.

Still flawless.

Miguel reached in, pulled out the gown’s storage box. The label on the side was his handwriting. Neat. Precise. Untraceable.

Clara’s voice dropped to a whisper. “Miguel.”

He didn’t look at her. “Heitor thinks he’s won.”

“He has.”

“Not yet.” Miguel closed the safe. Turned to face her. “Heitor took everything from me. My sister’s happiness. My reputation. My future. But he didn’t take *this*.”

Clara’s eyes flicked to the gown. “You can’t use it. Not without proof. Not without—”

Miguel cut her off. “I have proof. I’ve *always* had proof.”

Clara’s phone buzzed again. She ignored it. “Heitor’s moving faster than we thought. He’s cleaning house. He’s *erasing*.”

Miguel picked up the gown’s box. Held it against his chest. “Then he doesn’t have much time left.”

Clara’s breath hitched. “What does that mean?”

Miguel didn’t answer. He walked past her, toward the lab’s side door.

Clara grabbed his arm. “Miguel, wait.”

He stopped. Turned. Looked at her hand on his sleeve.

She let go. “Don’t go to her.”

“Valentina?”

“Yes.” Clara’s voice was raw. “Heitor’s already there. Or he will be soon. If you show up now—”

Miguel stepped back. “Then I’ll be where I need to be.”

Clara’s phone buzzed a third time. She pulled it out, her face draining of color as she read the message.

Miguel watched her. Watched the fear settle into her bones.

Clara looked up. “They found it.”

Miguel didn’t ask what *it* was.

Clara swallowed. “Heitor has the USB drive.”

Miguel felt a flicker of something in his chest. Not fear. Not surprise. Just the quiet understanding that the game had just changed.

Clara continued, voice trembling. “He knows it’s from you. He saw your handwriting on the label.”

Miguel turned toward the door. “Then he’s closer than I thought.”

Clara’s voice cracked. “You can’t outrun this. Not anymore.”

Miguel hesitated. Just for a second. Then he opened the door. The fluorescent lights of the costume workshop spilled into the lab, stark and cold.

He took a step forward.

Clara’s voice followed him. “Miguel. Stop.”

He didn’t stop.

The door swung shut behind him.

Clara stood alone in the lab, her breath ragged. She pulled out her phone again, not to read the message this time, but to dial a number. It rang once. Twice.

A voice answered. Low. Hesitant.

Clara spoke, her voice barely above a whisper. “He’s going to Valentina’s. He’s going to walk right into Heitor’s trap.”

A pause. Then: “No. He’s already in it.”

Chapter 12

The private vault at Ateliê Encanto was a small room carved into the studio’s basement, its walls lined with steel and monitored by a single security camera above the door. The air smelled of ozone and old fabric. Miguel stood just inside the threshold, the bridal gown’s case pressed against his chest like a shield.

Heitor Medeiros didn’t knock. The door burst open, hitting the stop with a metallic *thunk*. He stepped in without invitation, his tailored suit jacket flaring behind him. His cufflinks glinted under the recessed lighting.

Miguel didn’t move.

“You think hiding here changes anything?” Heitor’s voice was low, controlled. “You think those stitches and secrets will save you?”

Miguel turned the case in his hands, running his thumb over the embossed letters: *V.S.* in elegant script. He didn’t answer.

Heitor strode forward, stopping just short of the glass display case bolted to the center of the room. Inside lay the preserved wedding dress—cream silk shimmering under museum-grade lights. Unchanged. Untainted. Perfect.

Heitor’s jaw tightened. “You’ve been busy.”

Miguel set the case on the floor and knelt, unclasping the lid. The scent of lavender sachets and aged paper rose as he lifted the gown free. The fabric whispered against his fingers.

Heitor’s gaze locked onto the dress. “You didn’t destroy it.”

“I couldn’t.” Miguel folded the gown over his arm, letting the skirt pool at his feet. “It was the only truth left.”

“Truth?” Heitor let out a wet laugh. “You think a dress holds truth? It holds thread and dye and a bride’s humiliation. That’s all.”

Miguel didn’t react. He reached into his back pocket and pulled out a small black light. He flipped it on. A faint violet glow bathed the room.

Heitor’s eyes narrowed. “What is that?”

“Ultraviolet.” Miguel held the light over the gown’s bodice. The silk shimmered, unchanged. Then, in the folds of the skirt, along the seams and hidden stitches, a pattern emerged—not in color, but in *absence*. Thin, glowing lines where the fabric had been cut and re-stitched, repaired under surgical precision.

Heitor’s face darkened. “You’re lying.”

Miguel lowered the light. “Look closer.”

Heitor stepped forward, his polished shoe catching the hem of the gown. He didn’t lift it. Didn’t want to touch. Just stared at the glowing threads—thin as spider silk, but unmistakable.

“Sabotage,” Miguel said. “Your sabotage. Every stitch your team added, every thread they dyed with toxic pigment—it’s all here. Invisible unless you know what to look for.” He reached out, gently lifting a section of skirt. Beneath the silk, the original white fabric showed through—pristine. Unharmed. “You thought you’d ruined it. You thought the dress would turn red on her wedding day, that she’d be humiliated, that I’d be disgraced. But you didn’t account for *me*.”

Heitor’s fingers twitched. “This is a trick. A prop. A—”

“No.” Miguel refolded the gown, careful as a surgeon draping a patient. “This is evidence. And it’s going on the network tomorrow during the Master Designer gala. Every re-stitched seam. Every toxic thread. Every lie you built your empire on.”

Heitor lunged.

Miguel didn’t flinch. He stepped back, but the movement was fluid, practiced. The gown’s skirt caught Heitor’s wrist, yanking him off-balance. Heitor snarled, twisting, and in the struggle, the cuff of his sleeve snagged on a loose thread from the gown’s hem.

The fabric tore with a quiet *rip*.

Heitor staggered back, breathing hard, his cuff now frayed at the edge. He stared at the loose threads dangling from his sleeve—threads that matched the glowing pattern on the gown.

His eyes flicked to Miguel. Then to the door. Then back to the tear.

“You planned this,” he said, voice rough. “You let me touch it. You *wanted* to contaminate me.”

Miguel tucked the gown back into its case and stood. “I wanted you to see what you did.”

Heitor’s chest rose and fell, fast and shallow. The air between them thickened with something darker than anger—realization. Heitor finally understood.

And it was too late.

Miguel reached for the vault’s door handle.

Heitor’s voice cut through the silence like a blade. “You don’t know what you’ve done.”

Miguel paused. Turned.

Heitor’s face was pale, his usual composure shattered. “That dress isn’t just proof. It’s a *weapon*. And you’ve just handed it to the enemy.”

Miguel almost smiled. “I don’t have enemies anymore.”

Heitor’s eyes flickered toward the camera above the door. A single, knowing glance.

Then the lights in the vault flickered.

Once. Twice.

The emergency exit sign above the door buzzed, then died.

The camera above them whined, its red light blinking out.

Heitor’s expression shifted—something flickered across his face—not fear, but *anticipation*.

Miguel felt the shift in the air before the voice came over the vault’s intercom, smooth and calm:

“Senhor Medeiros. We have a situation.”

The door clicked open from the outside.

A man in a Rede Estrela Dourada security uniform stood in the doorway, his hand resting on his holster.

“Time’s up,” the guard said. “Both of you, come with me.”

Heitor didn’t move. Didn’t resist.

Miguel met the guard’s gaze. Held it.

Then he turned toward the gown’s case, knelt, and closed the lid.

The guard stepped forward, hand clamping onto Heitor’s shoulder. “You’ll want to see this,” he said, not to Heitor, but to Miguel.

Miguel didn’t ask what *this* was.

He stood, wiped his palms on his trousers, and walked toward the door—between the two men, as if he were the one in control.

Heitor let himself be led out first.

Miguel followed.

And as the vault door sealed behind them, the security feed in the hallway flickered once more—then went dark.

No sirens. No alarms.

Just silence.

And the distant sound of footsteps running down the corridor, fading into the bowels of Estúdio Aurora.

Chapter 13

The Grande Gala da Estrela Dourada filled the Estúdio Aurora’s main auditorium with gold and champagne. Velvet drapes swallowed the sound of forks clinking against crystal. Miguel sat in the fifth row, his hands folded on his lap. The program in his fingers bore Valentina’s name in bold print beneath the headline: REDEEMING THE BRIDE.

Across the aisle, Heitor Medeiros stood at the edge of the orchestra pit, his back to the audience, speaking in low tones to Dona Lúcia. His posture was regal—until his fingers drummed the railing like a metronome counting down to ruin.

A spotlight flared onstage. Valentina stepped into the cone of light wearing the original gown—simple, elegant, unchanged. The camera panned in as she lifted a single page of paper.

“I want to thank Rede Estrela Dourada for this opportunity,” she began, voice steady. “And especially Miguel Salgado, whose craftsmanship saved what was left of my life.”

A murmur rippled through the crowd. Heitor’s fingers stopped drumming.

Valentina continued. “Three months ago, someone tried to poison me. Not with arsenic or hemlock, but with a dye that would have turned my wedding gown red—literally—while I stood at the altar. My groom walked away. My reputation followed. But the real crime wasn’t mine. It was done to me by a man who believed his artistry gave him the right to destroy others.”

She held up a hand. The lights dimmed. A screen descended from the rafters.

“Tonight,” she said, “we show the truth.”

The first image flashed: Heitor in the costume workshop, leaning over a tray of swatches, his voice sharp. “Add the UV tracer to the lining. No one will see it under normal light, but when the cameras hit, the dye glows.”

Valentina didn’t flinch. She didn’t cry. She let the footage speak for her.

Cut to the wedding altar. The camera zoomed on the gown’s skirt as the toxic threads ignited under UV, the fabric bleeding scarlet in real time. The groom’s face twisted in disgust. The crowd gasped.

Cut back to Heitor, frozen in the archival footage, his mistake captured in high definition.

The auditorium held its breath. A woman in the front row clutched her pearls until they bit into her skin.

Valentina folded the paper, stepped offstage, and walked straight to Miguel’s row. She didn’t ask permission. She simply sat beside him, her skirt rustling against the velvet. The audience erupted in applause that rose and fell in waves.

Miguel didn’t look at her. His gaze stayed on the screen. On Heitor’s face, now flushed, his jaw clenched like a vice. Heitor turned once, scanning the crowd, and his eyes found Miguel—unblinking, unflinching. The look wasn’t anger. It was calculation.

“You planned this,” Valentina murmured, close enough that only Miguel could hear. “The timing. The footage. The gown’s reappearance.”

Miguel exhaled through his nose. “I planned nothing. I only ensured the world saw what he tried to hide.”

“You let him touch the evidence.”

“He needed to know he was caught.”

Valentina’s fingers tightened around her program. “He’ll retaliate.”

“Let him.”

She studied his profile—the stillness of his jaw, the faint pulse at his temple. “You’re not afraid of anything anymore, are you?”

Miguel finally turned his head. Their eyes met. Hers were clear. His showed nothing.

“Fear is a luxury for people who still have something to lose,” he said.

Valentina opened her mouth to respond, but the screen flickered. The lights surged back to full brightness. The footage cut to black. A hush fell.

Dona Lúcia stood at the podium, her usual poise cracked by something like betrayal. “Ladies and gentlemen, we have—”

The auditorium’s emergency lights burst on, harsh and red. A woman’s voice crackled over the PA: “There is a fire at Solar Medeiros. All guests are advised to evacuate.”

A gasp rippled through the crowd. Heads turned toward the emergency exits. Security guards moved in disciplined formation, flashlights cutting through the smoke haze that seeped from the ceiling vents.

Miguel stood. So did Valentina.

Heitor was nowhere to be seen.

Valentina grabbed Miguel’s wrist. “You have to leave. Now. Before they pin this on you.”

Miguel stared at the ceiling, where the sprinklers remained eerily silent. “This wasn’t me.”

“I know.” Her grip tightened. “But they won’t ask questions. They’ll assume.”

Footsteps pounded down the aisle behind them. Clara Vilela appeared, her face pale, her tablet clutched to her chest like a shield. “Miguel. You need to go. The police are locking down the exits. Heitor’s already being taken in, but—”

“But what?” Miguel asked.

Clara’s eyes darted toward the stage, then back. “But the footage from the vault—the one you showed Heitor—it’s missing. The security feed just looped static for seven minutes starting at 9:17.”

Miguel felt the weight of the moment settle into his bones. “He’s deleting his own trail.”

“No,” Clara said. “He’s making it yours.”

A hand clamped onto Miguel’s shoulder. A uniformed officer stood behind him, badge glinting. “Senhor Salgado. You’re coming with me.”

Miguel didn’t resist. He allowed himself to be turned, allowed the officer to guide him toward the nearest exit. Valentina followed, her heels clicking a rapid staccato on the marble floor.

Outside, the night air was thick with smoke. Solar Medeiros burned in the distance, flames licking the sky like orange tongues. The mansion’s iron gates glowed red, distorting the street signs beyond.

Heitor stood on the sidewalk, flanked by two officers, his suit jacket draped over his shoulders despite the heat. He looked disheveled, human. Broken.

Their eyes met across the distance. Heitor lifted one hand, fingers slightly curled—as if beckoning Miguel closer.

Miguel didn’t move.

Then Heitor smiled.

It wasn’t a kind smile. It wasn’t a cruel one. It was the smile of a man who had already calculated every possible outcome—and found one he could control.

The officer nudged Miguel forward. “Keep walking, senhor.”

They walked.

Heitor’s smile never wavered.

And then, as the first fire truck wailed in the distance, Heitor’s smile widened just enough for Miguel to see the glint of something small and metallic between his fingers—a sealed envelope, pressed to his palm.

He didn’t offer it. Didn’t explain.

Just watched Miguel pass by.

The envelope stayed with him.

The fire kept burning.

Chapter 14

The interrogation room at the 9th Precinct smelled like old coffee and disinfectant. Miguel sat on a wooden chair bolted to the floor, his wrists still cuffed loosely behind him. The air conditioning unit rattled in the corner, blowing dust onto his shoes.

Heitor entered without waiting for an invitation. His suit jacket was gone, his sleeves rolled to the elbows, revealing the same pale scars Miguel had glimpsed years ago when they’d both been junior designers at a small boutique in Itaim. Heitor carried a manila envelope in one hand and a plastic cup of water in the other.

Miguel didn’t look up.

Heitor set the cup on the metal table between them with a quiet click. “You always did have good manners. Even when you were sabotaging my life.”

Miguel finally lifted his head. “I didn’t sabotage anything. You did.”

Heitor pulled out the chair across from Miguel and sat, elbows on the table. His fingers drummed once, twice—then stilled. “You want to talk about sabotage? Fine. Let’s talk about the UV thread you sewed into Valentina’s gown. Let’s talk about the dress you preserved behind reinforced glass in a vault rigged with your own sensors. Let’s talk about the dye sample you took from my lab and replaced with inert powder. That’s not artistry. That’s war.”

Miguel exhaled through his nose. “And yet here we are. You’re the one in cuffs.”

Heitor leaned back, the chair creaking. “For now. But you should know something—you’re not the only one with leverage.”

From the envelope, he slid out a single sheet of paper and a USB drive. The resignation letter was typed in flawless Portuguese, dated that morning. The signature was unmistakable: Heitor Medeiros, Creative Director of Rede Estrela Dourada.

Miguel didn’t touch it. “You’re quitting? After everything?”

Heitor tapped the paper. “I’m not quitting. I’m ending the game on my terms.” He pushed the USB drive forward with a knuckle. “Inside this is everything you need to bury me professionally. Confessions. Transfers. Evidence of fraud. Of embezzlement. Of—well, you know.”

Miguel’s pulse didn’t change. He kept his hands still. “And the price?”

“You withdraw your claims. You drop the sabotage suit. You let the gala scandal fade. You walk away from Valentina’s testimony.” Heitor’s voice dropped. “You never mention Solar Medeiros. Never. And in return, I disappear. No trial. No scandal. Just a quiet exit.”

Miguel studied the resignation letter. The signature trembled slightly, the only sign of strain. “And the dress?”

Heitor’s smile was thin. “Still yours. But if you ever use it against me, the USB gets mailed to every major news outlet in Brazil. Including the unedited footage of your sister’s wedding—where she was *not* the victim you claim.”

A beat of silence. The air conditioning wheezed.

Miguel reached for the envelope. Heitor didn’t pull it back. Their fingers didn’t touch.

“And your father?” Miguel asked. “Your family name? You’d let them take the fall for this?”

Heitor’s eyes darkened. “My father made his choices decades ago. I’m making mine now.”

Miguel took the envelope. He slipped the resignation letter and the USB into his pocket. Then he stood.

The cuffs clinked as he turned toward the door. The officer outside cracked it open, ready to lead him out.

Heitor spoke just before Miguel stepped into the hall. “You’re not a monster, Miguel. That’s why you’ll fail.”

Miguel paused. He didn’t turn around. “I’ve been sewing monsters since I was twelve. I know exactly what they look like.”

Outside, the hallway stretched long and fluorescent. Miguel walked with his shoulders level, his stride even. He didn’t look back.

He didn’t hear the officer behind him inhale sharply. Didn’t see Heitor’s smile tighten as two uniformed officers guided him toward the holding cells.

Miguel only felt the weight of the envelope in his pocket. Solid. Final.

He passed a bulletin board cluttered with missing persons flyers and arrest warrants. A shadow flickered at the end of the hall—Sofia Prado, leaning against a vending machine, arms crossed. She wore a dark blazer and a scowl that could curdle milk.

Miguel stopped.

She pushed off the machine and walked toward him, her heels clicking on the linoleum. When she was close enough that her perfume—jasmine and cigarette smoke—cut through the stale air, she spoke.

“You just made a deal with the devil.”

Miguel kept walking. “I made a deal with a desperate man.”

“Desperate men lie.”

“So do journalists.”

She matched his pace, falling into step beside him. “I wasn’t lying when I said I’d expose him. You just handed him a get-out-of-jail-free card.”

Miguel reached the exit. A desk sergeant nodded at him. The door buzzed open.

Sofia didn’t follow. She stopped at the threshold, framed by the harsh morning light.

“You really think this ends here?” she asked.

Miguel stepped into the sunlight. The heat hit him like a warning.

He turned once, just enough to catch her reflection in the glass door.

“No,” he said. “This isn’t the end. It’s just the first stitch.”

Chapter 15

The Grand Salon of the Casa das Rosas was drowning in gold.

Gilded mirrors lined the walls, catching the spill of champagne flutes and camera flashes. Crystal chandeliers cast prismatic light over the elite of Rede Estrela Dourada—network executives in jewel-toned silks, designers in leather-bound portfolios, journalists with recorders tucked like daggers into lapel pockets. The air hummed with the clink of crystal, the murmur of arranged deals, the rise and fall of carefully modulated laughter.

Miguel stood near the back, adjusting the cuff of his charcoal suit. His fingers moved with surgical precision, tucking the last loose thread beneath the sleeve’s seam. He hadn’t worn a tuxedo since Valentina’s wedding—since the night his sister’s gown had turned red under the stage lights, exposing Heitor’s poison.

A waiter paused beside him, silver tray extended. “Champagne, senhor?”

Miguel took a flute. The liquid inside shimmered, catching the light like a promise. He didn’t drink.

Sofia Prado leaned against a pillar near the entrance, notebook in hand, her dark eyes scanning the room. She wore a sleeveless emerald dress that clung to her frame, the fabric shifting when she moved. A Nikon hung from her neck, its strap tight against her collarbone. She hadn’t approached him yet.

Miguel turned away before she could spot him. His gaze locked onto the stage, where a long table draped in red velvet awaited the night’s honors. At its center stood a single chair, upholstered in the same fabric as the table runner—a deep crimson, the hue of Valentina’s ruined gown.

The MC, a perky brunette in a sequined jumpsuit, tapped the microphone. “Senhores e senhoras! Tonight, we celebrate the artistry of Rede Estrela Dourada!”

The room hushed. Miguel took a slow breath.

“First, let’s honor our outgoing Creative Director, Heitor Medeiros.”

The screens above the stage flickered to life, projecting a montage of Heitor’s greatest productions: sweeping ballroom gowns, military epaulettes, a bridal veil stained red in a failed stain test. The audience clapped politely. Miguel didn’t.

“And now,” the MC continued, “the moment you’ve all been waiting for. Rede Estrela Dourada is proud to present the title of Master Designer to…”

She paused. A calculated beat.

Miguel’s stomach tightened.

“…Miguel Salgado!”

The room erupted. Flashbulbs popped like firecrackers. Hands slapped his back as he was pulled toward the stage. He moved with quiet determination, weaving through the crowd, his shoes silent on the polished marble.

Sofia caught his arm just before the steps. “Wait.”

He stopped.

She held out a small device—an audio recorder, its red light blinking. “You don’t have to do this.”

Miguel didn’t take it. “I do.”

“Heitor’s gone. The evidence is gone. What’s left?”

“My name. My work.” He met her gaze. “And the right to decide where this ends.”

She exhaled through her nose. “You’re not just accepting a title. You’re accepting a throne.”

Miguel stepped onto the stage. The spotlight hit him full force, turning his skin to gold. He ascended the dais, nodding at the applause, his expression unreadable.

The MC handed him a microphone. “Master Designer—your speech!”

Miguel adjusted the stand. “Thank you.”

The room hushed, anticipating gratitude, humility.

Instead, he said, “Tonight isn’t about me.”

A murmur rippled through the crowd. The MC’s smile flickered.

Miguel gestured to the seamstresses and tailors standing along the walls—more than twenty of them, each in simple black shifts, each clutching a small envelope. “It’s about them.”

He signaled. The seamstresses stepped forward. One by one, they opened their envelopes and held up a document: a contract for healthcare, pensions, and profit-sharing from Rede Estrela Dourada.

The room erupted again—but this time, it wasn’t applause.

Sofia’s recorder whirred. She raised the Nikon, her eyes sharp behind the lens.

Miguel didn’t look at her. “We are artists,” he said, “not indentured labor. Tonight, we begin changing that.”

A producer in the front row shouted, “This isn’t part of the program!”

Miguel smiled—small, private. “No. It wasn’t.”

The MC regained her composure. “Master Designer Salgado, a bold move.”

“It’s not bold,” Miguel said. “It’s necessary.”

He stepped down from the stage, leaving the microphone to crackle into silence. The seamstresses followed him, their heads high, their envelopes clutched like shields.

Sofia was waiting at the edge of the crowd. She lowered the camera. “You just declared war on your own network.”

Miguel walked past her. “I declared independence.”

She matched his pace. “And what about the dress? The USB? Heitor’s leverage?”

Miguel didn’t answer. He reached the main doors, pushed through, and stepped into the cool night air of Jardins. The city hummed beyond the walls, indifferent.

Sofia followed him onto the sidewalk. “Miguel.”

He stopped. Turned.

She held out the audio recorder. “You should have this.”

He took it. Their fingers brushed. A spark—nothing more.

She said, “Your speech was brilliant. But it’s not over.”

He clipped the recorder into his pocket. “I know.”

Sofia lit a cigarette, cupping the flame against the wind. “Heitor’s gone, but your sister’s still out there. Valentina’s film is still missing. And your vault…”

Miguel’s mouth tightened.

She exhaled smoke. “You’re not safe. Not yet.”

Miguel looked at the sky. The stars were faint over São Paulo’s glow. “I never asked for safety.”

A car idled at the curb—a sleek Audi, tinted windows. The driver stepped out, opened the rear door.

Sofia flicked ash onto the pavement. “Where to now?”

Miguel got into the car. The door clicked shut.

Sofia tapped the window. “Miguel.”

He rolled it down.

She leaned in. “You’re making a mistake trusting the wrong people.”

He met her eyes. “I’ve never trusted the right ones.”

The car pulled away.

Sofia stayed on the sidewalk, watching taillights vanish into the traffic. She crushed the cigarette under her heel, then lit another.

The streetlamps flickered once, twice—like a dying pulse.

She didn’t notice the seamstress watching from the shadows of the Casa das Rosas—small, silent, her thimble glinting in the dark.
